completeness.rationale: All seven expected information items were extracted: intermittent ~1-in-3 rate, started ~2 days ago, coincides with deployment/role change, analyst role specifically affected, other roles unaffected (anecdotally confirmed), multiple users affected, and load-balanced multi-server environment inferred from the failure rate pattern.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis precisely matches both the canonical cause and an acceptable diagnostic path. The agent correctly identified: inconsistent deployment across load-balanced servers, the analyst role as the specific vector, the ~1-in-3 pattern implying one of three servers is misconfigured, and the timing correlation with the deployment. The summary even correctly infers '3 backend instances with 1 misconfigured' from the failure rate.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent methodically worked through: timing/trigger, error page origin (app vs. proxy), session/timing patterns, deployment correlation, role specificity, and cross-team affected population. No obvious follow-up questions were left unasked given the reporter's technical limitations.

economy.rationale: The question about session timing (whether it happens more after being away) was somewhat low-value — the intermittent-on-refresh pattern already pointed away from session issues. All other questions were well-targeted and productive. One minor inefficiency in an otherwise tight conversation.

question_quality.rationale: The questions demonstrated genuine diagnostic reasoning. Asking whether the error page was TaskFlow-branded vs. generic was insightful (differentiating app-layer vs. proxy-layer 403). Pushing back on 'truly random' and asking about frequency patterns elicited the critical '1-in-3' observation. Connecting the deployment timing to the analyst role assignment was the pivotal question that unlocked the root cause.

actionability.rationale: The summary includes a precise root cause hypothesis, reproduction steps, specific recommended fix steps (verify deployment status per server, compare RBAC config, redeploy to stale server), a proposed test case for verification, and clearly scoped information gaps. A developer could begin investigating and coding a fix immediately.

notable_strengths: Elicited the '1-in-3' failure rate from a reporter who attributed it to randomness — this was the key structural clue and the agent drew it out skillfully
Connected the analyst role assignment email to the deployment timing in a single well-framed question, unlocking the root cause
Correctly inferred '3 backend servers, 1 misconfigured' from the ~33% failure rate without the reporter having infrastructure knowledge
Triage summary is exceptionally actionable with reproduction steps, fix recommendations, and a proposed test case
notable_weaknesses: The session/timing question (whether 403s correlate with time away from the app) added a turn without meaningful diagnostic value given the refresh behavior already ruled out session expiry
most_insightful_question: Do you know if there were any deployments, updates, or infrastructure changes to TaskFlow around the time this started a couple days ago? — This question, combined with the follow-up about the analyst role, was the pivot point that connected the symptoms to the actual cause.
missed_opportunities: Could have directly asked whether the reporter recently had their role changed (not just whether others were affected), which would have surfaced the analyst role assignment earlier
Did not ask whether the 403 response included any request ID or server identifier in headers, which could have confirmed the load-balancer hypothesis with a single data point
